coredata.dc:title: Alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogel for cancer detection and treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Mingliang
Jia, Xu
Zhao, Xubo
Li, Jiagen
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogels were designed for the tumor diagnosis and chemotherapy, by crosslinking the folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (FA-PEG-NH2) and rhodamine B (RhB)-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (RhB-PEG-NH2) modified oxidized alginate (OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB) with cystamine (Cys), followed covalent conjugation of doxorubicin (DOX) via acid-labile Schiff base bond. Owing to the surface folic acid (FA) groups, disulfide crosslinking structure and Schiff base conjugation for DOX, the folate receptor (FR)-mediated targeting and pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered release of DOX was achieved. The cytotoxicity and cellular uptake results clearly illustrated that most DOX was released and accumulated in the cell nuclei and killed the cancer cells efficaciously, due to the desirable targeting intracellular triggered release. Furthermore, the theranostic nanogels could be used for the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells, owing to the pH-modulated fluorescence property of the pendant RhB groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Theranostic prodrug nanogels
FR-mediated targeting
Intracellular triggered release
Fluorescent imaging
Alginate

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of rhodamine B-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (RhB-PEG-NH2)
Synthesis of oxidized alginate modified with folate-terminated and rhodamine B-terminated poly (ethylene glycol) (OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB)
Preparation and characterization of multifunctional alginate-based nanogels
Preparation and characterization of theranostic prodrug nanogels

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was granted financial support from the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University , Ministry of Education of China (Grant no. NCET-09-0441 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn on theranostic prodrug nanogels, and their intracellular triggering behavior in tumor cells.
FT-IR spectra of (a) OAL, (b) OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB, (c) COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB, (d) DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels.
TEM images of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels before (a) and after treating in 10mM GSH for 12h (b).
Typical hydrodynamic diameter distributions of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels before (a) and after treating in 10mM GSH for 12h (b), and the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels (c).

(a) Ultraviolet absorbance spectra of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels (0.1mg/mL) as a function of the different media pH values. Inset: Digital photos of the dispersion of the nanogels (0.1mg/mL) at pH 4.0 (left) and water (right) at daylight lamp and 365nm UV lamp; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra (excitation at λex=500nm) of the dispersions of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels (0.1mg/mL) at different pH values..
In vitro DOX release profiles from the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug at different pH values. at different media. The data were given as the mean±SD (n=3).
Cytotoxicity of free DOX (a) and the multi-functional COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels and DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels (b) by the MTT assay. The data were given as the mean±SD (n=3).
Cellular uptake of HepG2 cells stained by Hoechst (a) and incubation with the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels (100μg/mL) for 12h (b) by fluorescence microscope technique.

head.ce:title: Alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogel for cancer detection and treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogels were designed for the tumor diagnosis and chemotherapy, by crosslinking the folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (FA-PEG-NH2) and rhodamine B (RhB)-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (RhB-PEG-NH2) modified oxidized alginate (OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB) with cystamine (Cys), followed covalent conjugation of doxorubicin (DOX) via acid-labile Schiff base bond. Owing to the surface folic acid (FA) groups, disulfide crosslinking structure and Schiff base conjugation for DOX, the folate receptor (FR)-mediated targeting and pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered release of DOX was achieved. The cytotoxicity and cellular uptake results clearly illustrated that most DOX was released and accumulated in the cell nuclei and killed the cancer cells efficaciously, due to the desirable targeting intracellular triggered release. Furthermore, the theranostic nanogels could be used for the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells, owing to the pH-modulated fluorescence property of the pendant RhB groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Theranostic nanoparticles, which combine both therapeutic and diagnostic functions in one dose, has promise to propel the biomedical field toward personalized medicine, especially in cancer treatment (Ma, Huang, Song, Chen, & Zhang 2016). As natural biological macromolecules, besides the benefits of diversity in size and charge, biodegradable and abundant and low toxicity in vivo (Uthaman, Maya, Jayakumar, Cho, & Park, 2014), polysaccharides have numerous advantages for theranostics: the integrated approach of therapeutics and diagnostics. Their derivable reactive groups can be leveraged for functionalization with a nanoparticle-enabling conjugate, therapeutics (small molecules, proteins, peptides, photosensitizers) and/or diagnostic agents (imaging agents, sensors) (Swierczewska, han, Kim, Park, & Lee, 2016). Furthermore, their stimuli sensitivity makes the engineering polysaccharide-based nanogels smart theranostic nanomedicine (Maya et al., 2013).

Natural polysaccharides could easily form biodegradable nanogels, owning good stability in biological fluids due to the existence of the low driving forces for their aggregation. Compared to other nanocarriers, nanogels usually have good biocompatibility, high aqueous dispersibility, and well-defined stable structure (Li, Maciel, Rodrigues, Shi, & Tomas 2015).Their flexibility and softness may allow their easier penetration ability through human skin while maintaining the bioactivity of the therapeutics in comparison with those of the corresponding rigid nanoparticles. Additionally, their flexibility can also help prolong the circulating lifetime through reducing the possibility of their entrapment by macrophages. Nanogels have been proven to be more efficiently taken up by cells than conventional nanocarriers such as liposomes, which are less stable, resulting in an improved bioavailability and safety of the therapeutics in vivo.

Alginate is one of the most abundant biodegradable natural linear polysaccharide derived from gulfweed, bacteria or seaweed of brown algae, composed of 1–4 linked α-l-guluronic (G) and β-d-mannuronic (M) acid residues. It has been recognized in biomedical applications, owing to its non-antigenicity, nontoxicity, satisfactory biocompatibility, favorable biodegradability, and pH sensitivity (Lee & Mooney, 2012). The engineering alginate-based nanogels have been reported as smart theranostic nanomedicine in the most recent years. For examples, Su and Cheng designed the highly biocompatible and safe nanocomposites composed of alginate and iron oxide nanoparticles to encapsulate doxorubicine for brain tumor therapy, which also allowed for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (Su & Cheng, 2015). Podgorna et al. prepared the gadolinium alginate gel nanoparticles (GdNG) by the reverse microemulsion and physical crosslinking method as the vehicles able to carry hydrophilic drugs and to be traced by the MRI (Podgorna et al., 2017). Lengert et al. fabricated silver alginate hydrogel microcapsules serve as microcontainer with a functional substance payload, which also demonstrated the possibility to enhance the Raman signal of up to 105 fold: a magnitude which is in the range for applications in theranostics (Lengert et al., 2017).

Additionally, the cis-diol groups in alginate could be oxidized, usually resulting into the cleavage of glucosidic bonds, and the resultant lower molecular-weight alginate is good for excretion (Yang, Xie, & He, 2011). In the oxidation process, two aldehyde groups are simultaneously created, enabling more modification feasibilities (Pawar & Edgar, 2012). Furthermore, the created acetal groups are susceptible to hydrolysis, endowing the oxidized alginate (OAL) tunable degradation rates (Boontheekul, Kong, & Mooney, 2005). So the OAL, which could be designed with controllable chain length and more derivable reactive groups (inherent carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, as well as created aldehyde group), shows promising potential in smart theranostic nanomedicine. Our group has designed mPEG-OAL-DOX/Cdots theranostic nanoparticles for imaging-guided drug delivery in tumor treatment, by conjugating DOX via acid-labile Schiff base linkage onto mPEG-OAL/Cdots nanoparticles that were prepared by cross-linking the PEGylated oxidized alginate (mPEG-OAL) with fluorescent carbon dots (Cdots) (Jia et al., 2016a). In these reported theranostics nanoparticles based on alginate or OAL, the diagnostic agents could generate signals in both physiological and tumor media.

Nowadays, the cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on nanotheranostics have been proposed with the capability of selectively delivering therapeutic reagents into cancer cells or tumor tissues while simultaneously generating unique signals that can be readily monitored under both in vitro and in vivo conditions (Li, Kim, Yoon, & Chen, 2017). That is to say, the diagnostic agents could only generate unique signals in the tumor tissues, which could be readily used for the real-time monitoring of the tumor tissues. For example, the nanotheranostics bearing pH-dependent fluorescent Rhodamine 6G group only possessed strong fluorescence in acidic tumor microenvironment, with very weak intensity in the physiological medium (Zhao et al., 2016). Besides, it has been demonstrated that the cancer-targeted nanotheranostics could enhance the selective targeting to tumors expressing high levels of folate receptor (FR), by conjugation of folate to the nanotheranostics (Badruddoza et al., 2013; Jia et al., 2016b).

Based on the multi-functionalities of OAL, we hypothesize that novel promising cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogel could be designed via all-in-one functionalization of OAL. Inspired by the hypothesis, alginate-based multi-functional theranostic prodrug nanogels were designed for the first time in the present work by crosslinking the folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (FA-PEG-NH2) and rhodamine B (RhB)-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (RhB-PEG-NH2) modified oxidized alginate (OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB) with cystamine (Cys), followed covalent conjugation of DOX via acid-labile Schiff base bond (Scheme 1 ). In the hypothesis, the designed theranostic prodrug nanogels should be stable in blood stream with low toxic and side effect because of their minimal DOX-leakage owing to the PEGylation and the acid-cleavable covalent conjugation of DOX. And they could express very weak fluorescence in such neutral media. Then they targeted in tumor tissues via EPR effect, and could be uptaken by tumor cells via FR-mediated endocytosis. As in the reductant and acidic media in the tumor microenvironment, both the disulfide crosslinking structure and Schiff base conjugation for DOX should be cleaved off, realizing the pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered DOX release. Furthermore, the pendant RhB groups would express strong fluorescence in such acidic media, which could be used the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells. Therefore, the cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogels showed promising potential for cancer detection and treatment.

Sodium alginate (AL, Chemical pure, M/G=0.50, M n =35,000 and M w =218,000) was purchased from Xudong Chem. Co. Ltd., Beijing, China. Poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (H2N-PEG2000-NH2, Mn
=2000, 99%) was provided by Beijing Kaizheng Biotech Development Co. Ltd.

Folic acid (FA, 97%) and rhodamine B (AR) were got from Tianjin Guangfu Technology and Development Co. Ltd. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) was obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Low molecular weight 1-(3-dimethylaminoproyl)-3-ethylcarbodiinide hydrochloride (EDC∙HCl, 99%) was purchased from J& K Chemical Co. Ltd. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, 99.4%) was provided from Beijing Huafang United Technology Co. Ltd. Glutathione (GSH, 97%) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Shanghai, China. All other reagents were analytical grade and used directly. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Oxidized alginate (OAL) was synthesized by the periodate oxidation method as reported previously (Mu, Lu, & Liu, 2011). Typically, sodium alginate (3.000g) was dissolved in 300mL deionized water with vigorous stirring in the dark for 12h to obtain a homogeneous solution. 30mL sodium periodate (NaIO4, 2.000g, 9.35mmol, 62 mol% relative to the alginate units) aqueous solution was added into and stirred for 24h in the dark at room temperature. The product was separated though dialysis (MWCO of 14000) against water for 3days in the dark and subsequently lyophilization. The degree of oxidation (DO%, defined as the number of oxidized alginate units per 100 alginate units) of OAL was analyzed as about 20%, by determining the concentration of unconsumed periodate after 6h by iodometry (Luan et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rhodamine B-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (RhB-PEG-NH2)
ce:section.ce:para:
Rhodamine B (0.2395g, 0.50mmol) was dissolved in 15mL of anhydrous dichloromethane. After phosphorus oxychloride (0.14mL, 1.49mmol) was added within 5min, the mixture was refluxed with stirring for about 4h until colorless. The compound was obtained via rotary evaporation and dissolved in 35mL acetonitrile. The solution was added in 25mL NH2-PEG2000-NH2 (1.000g, 0.50mmol) solution in acetonitrile containing drops of tirethylamine with vigorous stirring within 2h, and then the reaction mixture was stirred for 24h at room temperature. The red solid was obtained via precipitation in anhydrous ether in ice both. The product was purified though three cycles of dissolution in dichloromethane and precipitation in anhydrous ether, finally dried in vacuum at 40°C (Li, Zhang, Hu, Cheng, & Liu 2010). 1H NMR (DMSO-d6, δ, ppm) 8.62 (NC H -), 6.63-7.66 (C H of aromatic ring), 3.50 (PEG, −CH2C H
2O-), 3.34 (PEG, −C H
2CH2O-), 4.48 (-N H CH2-), 2.69 (-NHC H
2-), 0.99–1.73 (-C H
2CH2-,-N H
2) (Fig. S1). The graft degree of RhB-PEG-NH2 was determined as 42.3% from the 1H NMR analysis.

Triethylamine (0.11mL, 0.79mmol) and folic acid (0.2207g, 0.5mmol) were dissolved in 10mL anhydrous DMSO with vigorous stirring. Then, DCC (0.1032g, 0.5mmol) and NSH (0.0575g, 0.5mmol) were added in the solution and the mixture was stirred for 8h at room temperature in the dark. After removing the generated precipitate (dicyclohexylurea, DCU) by the filtration, 10mL DMSO solution of NH2-PEG2000-NH2 (2.000g, 0.50mmol) was added into the filtrate and the reaction was preceded for 48h at room temperature. Subsequently, the product was purified via dialysis (MWCO of 1000) against distilled water for 3days to remove off any impurity and organic solvent. Finally, the product of FA-PEG-NH2 was obtained by lyophilization as a yellow solid (Pasut et al., 2008). 1H NMR (DMSO-d 6, δ, ppm): 6.33-7.50 (C H of aromatic ring), 3.50 (PEG, −CH2C H
2O-), 3.32 (PEG, −C H
2CH2O-), 2.69 (-NHC H
2CH3), 0.97-1.97 (C H
3CH2-,-N H
2) (Fig. S1). The graft degree of FA-PEG-NH2 was determined as 71.2% from the 1H NMR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxidized alginate modified with folate-terminated and rhodamine B-terminated poly (ethylene glycol) (OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB)
ce:section.ce:para: NHS (14.0mg, 0.122mmol) was added in 20mL solution of OAL (0.2212g, 1.117mmol) in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 5.5). After stirring for 15min, EDC∙CCI (43.0mg, 0.224mmol) was added to activate the carboxyl groups with vigorous stirring for 30min. Then 5.0mL aqueous solution containing FA-PEG-NH2 (0.2036g, 0.084mmol) and RhB-PEG-NH2 (0.2082g, 0.084mmol) was dropwise added. The pH value of the mixture was adjusted to 6.0 with NaOH solution (0.1mol/L), and the mixture was stirred at room temperature in the dark for 2days. Finally, the product of OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB was purified to completely remove the residual byproducts and raw materials through dialysis (MWCO of 3500) against distilled water for 3days, and finally collected by lyophilization (Zhao et al., 2014).

OAL-g-PEG-RhB/FA (80mg) was dissolved in 15mL of pH 6.5 PBS, and then NHS (14.0mg, 0.122mmol) was added with stirring for 15min. Subsequently, EDC (46.8mg, 0.244mmol) was added into the solution to activate the remained carboxyl groups in OAL-g-PEG-RhB/FA for 30min. Then cystamine dihydrochloride (Cys) (24.8mg, 0.110mmol) was added into the mixture with stirring at room temperature for 2days. The mixture was adjusted to pH 5.5 with NaOH solution (0.1mol/L). Finally, the products, COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels, were purified via dialysis (MWCO=3500) against deionized water for 3days, and collected via lyophilization.

COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels (5.0mg) and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, 3.0mg) were dispersed into a mixed solvent (containing 3.33mL of water and 1.67mL of methanol) with ultrasonic vibration. Then 20μL of acetic anhydride was added as a catalyst with stirring. After adjusting to pH 7.4 with NaOH solution (0.1mol/L), the dispersion was stirred magnetically for 48h at room temperature in the dark. Finally the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels were collected via centrifugation (12000rpm for 10min), washed repeatedly with pH 7.4 PBS, and dried in vacuum.

The DOX concentrations in all the fluid supernatant were determined to calculate the conjugated DOX, by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 480nm. The DOX content in the prodrug nanogels were calculated as the mass ratio between the conjugated DOX and the total mass of the prodrug nanogels.

The cytotoxicity of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels and the final DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels against HepG2 cells was evaluated using the MTT assay. The HepG2 cells were implanted into the 96-well plate with the density 1×104 cells per well in 100μL DMEM containing 10% FBS and cultured for 1day at 37°C in 5% CO2 atmosphere. The nanogels or prodrug nanogels were seeded into wells with different concentrations. After 48h incubation, the culture medium was disposed and washed thrice with PBS (0.01mol/L, pH 7.4). Then, 20μL MTT liquor (5mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well and 150μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the crystal. Cell viability was measured with a microplate reader at a wavelength of 490nm.

The cellular uptake of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels was observed with a fluorescence microscope (DM 4000B) after 12h incubation to visualize the intercellular distribution of DOX in HepG2 cells. The cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33258. The location of cellular fluorescence was validated with excitation wavelength of 480nm (DOX) and 405nm (Hoechst 33258), respectively.

The in vitro DOX release from the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels was conducted in four different buffer solutions comprehensively (pH 7.4-PBS; pH 7.4-PBS/10μM GSH; pH 5.0-ABS (acetate buffer solution); or pH 5.0-ABS/10mM GSH) at 37°C. The dispersion of the prodrug nanogels in the corresponding buffer (10.0mg/mL, 10.0mL) was poured in a membrane dialysis bag with MWCO of 14000. The bag was immersed in 110mL of buffer at 37°C while being shaken at 120rpm. The solution outside the dialysis bag (5mL) was collected at the predetermined time points and the same volume of fresh buffer was added. The DOX content in the solution was analyzed using the UV–vis spectrophotometer at 480nm. Each sample of cumulative release profiles were plotted versus release time. All the experiments were carried out in duplicate and the data presented are the average of two measurements. The cumulative release rate was estimated by the formulae as shown below:
A c c u m u l a t i v e
r e l e a s e
r a t e
( % ) =
C t
V o
+
V ′
Σ
i = 1
t − 1
M
t o t a l
× 100 %
where Mtotal represented the total mass of the conjugated DOX in the prodrug nanogels prior to release. Vo and V' represented initial release volume and the preconcerted time interval of collected volume (Vo =120mL, V'=5mL), respectively. Ct represented the concentration of DOX in each sample by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 35 UV–vis).
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by using a Bruker IFS 66v/s infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany).
1H NMR spectra were characterized with a Nuclear Magnetic Resonance instrument (JEOL ECS 400M) with dimethylsulfoxide-D6 or D2O as solvent.
The steady-state fluorescent emission spectra of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels were detected in their aqueous dispersions with a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer.

The morphology of the nanogels was examined with a JEM-1200 EX/S transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The samples were dispersed in double distilled water with the aid of ultrasonication for 3h at first, drops of the uniform dispersed solution were deposited on a copper grid covered with a perforated carbon film in sequence, and then evaporated the solvent.

The mean hydrodynamic diameter and size distribution of the samples were determined by a dynamic light scattering system (BI-200SM, Brookhaven Instruments) using 135mW intense laser excitation at 514.5nm at a detection angle of 90° at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of multifunctional alginate-based nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, the alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn on theranostic prodrug nanogels were prepared with the procedure depicted in Scheme 1. Firstly, oxidized alginate (OAL) was synthesized via oxidation with sodium periodate as strong oxidant to acquire aldehyde groups for successive chemical modification. To endow the theranostic prodrug nanogels tumor-targeting and visual tracking characteristics, FA-PEG-NH2 and RhB-PEG-NH2 were grafted with the OAL backbone via the carbodiimide chemistry. Then the OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB was crosslinked to achieve the bioreducibly degradable COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels with cystamine as crosslinking reagent. Finally, the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels were obtained after conjugating DOX onto the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels via acid-cleavable Schiff base linkage.

The FT-IR spectra of OAL, OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB, COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB, and DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB prodrug nanogels are presented in Fig. 1 . Compared with the spectrum of OAL, a new characteristic absorbance at 1107cm−1 of stretching vibration of COC bond appeared in the product after amidation reaction (Lee et al., 2016). Furthermore, the CH2 stretching vibration absorbance at 2875cm−1 increased. The results revealed the successful synthesis of OAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB. In the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S1), the chemical shift at δ=3.62ppm assigned to the methylene protons in OCH2CH2 groups (Lee et al., 2016) and the one at δ=6.3–7.6ppm of protons in aromatic rings revealed the successful functionalization of OAL with FA-PEG-NH2 and RhB-PEG-NH2 (Fathi et al., 2017). In additional, the chemical shifts (b and c) at 2.85ppm and 2.62ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB, which is belonged to the protons in cystamine (Cheewatanakornkool, Niratisai, Manchun, Dass, & Sriamornsak, 2017), indicated the successful crosslinking with cystamine.

The particle size and distribution of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels were characterized with TEM and DLS techniques. The COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels exhibited particle size of 30–40nm in TEM analysis, with uniform and regular spherical morphology, and consistent with the narrow size distribution (Fig. 2 a). And they gave an average hydrodynamic diameter of 250nm in the water solution from the DLS analysis (Fig. 3 a), due to swelling in water. To reveal the reduction-responsive property of the nanogels under pathological media, the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels were dispersed in 10mmol/L GSH. After stirring for 12h, their particle size distribution became wide in the range of 10–200nm, still keeping the spherical morphology (Fig. 2b). In the DLS analysis result (Fig. 3b), there were two hydrodynamic diameter ranges, a small one ≪100nm and a big one in 350–650nm. The results showed that the disulfide crosslinkages in the nanogels had been cleaved off by GSH, and the nanogels were subsequently broken into small pieces (Zhang, Jia, Pei, & Liu, 2017). Simultaneously, some small pieces aggregated into big ones. The results demonstrated the excellent bioreducible degradation of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels, which could be used for the reduction-responsive drug vehicle for controlled release.

UV-vis spectra were also used to reveal the successful modification of FA and RhB groups on the OAL backbones. Two uniform characteristic peaks appeared around 280nm and 580nm, attributing to the absorbance of FA and RhB groups, respectively (Fig. 4 ). From the UV–vis spectra, the FA and RhB content in the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels could be calculated as 12.4% and 0.28% respectively, by determining the intensity of each characteristic absorbance using a series of standard FA or rhodamine B solution as calibration. Furthermore, the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels expressed red fluorescence under 365nm UV (insert in Fig. 4A, dispersion of the nanogels (0.1mg/mL) at pH 4.0 aqueous solution (left)), while neither color nor fluorescence could be seen for the dispersion in water (insert in Fig. 4A, dispersion of the nanogels (0.1mg/mL) in water (right)). Furthermore, the absorbance curves at different pH media also showed that the dispersion expressed different fluorescent intensities, with stronger fluorescent intensity at lower pH media. The results demonstrated that the potential application of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels for the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells, because of the pH difference in normal physiological medium (near neutral) and tumor microenvironment (acidic media).

To further investigate the pH-modulated fluorescence of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels at different pH media, the steady-state fluorescent emission spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. 4B. The fluorescent intensity decreased with increasing the pH value of the dispersion. Stronger fluorescence was achieved at the media with pH ≪6. Especially, when pH value increased to approximately 6.0, the dispersion appeared non-fluorescent due to the fact that the RhB segment in the nanogels is in its spiro ring structure (Jia et al., 2016c). It further indicated the potential application of the proposed nanogels in the fluorescent imaging for tumor diagnosis, with the cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of theranostic prodrug nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: After conjugation of DOX, a new characterized absorbance appeared of 1720cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of CN bond in the FT-IR spectrum of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels (Fig. 1), demonstrating the successful covalent conjugation of DOX via Schiff base bond. By detecting the DOX concentration in the fluid supernatant after drug conjugation with UV–vis spectrometry at 480nm, the DOX content could be calculated as 36.7% ±1.06%, with drug encapsulation efficiency (DEE) of 25.80%±1.29%. Such high DOX content in the proposed prodrug nanogels is beneficial to their application in cancer chemotherapy, in which desirable anticancer efficiency is expected with low dosage of the proposed prodrug nanogels.

The high DOX content in the prodrug nanogels was also revealed by the DLS analysis (Fig. 2(c)). After conjugation of DOX, the average hydrodynamic diameter increased from 250nm of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels to 380nm of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels.

The pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered release of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels was compared in different media: pH 7.4 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS with 10μM GSH (mimicking the normal physiological media), pH 5.0 ABS, and pH 5.0 ABS with 10mM GSH (mimicking the tumor micro-environment). All the controlled release profiles exhibited no obvious burst release at the two pH values regardless of GSH. And the cumulative release at pH 5.0 or with GSH was higher than that at pH 7.4 or without GSH (Fig. 5 ), demonstrating obvious pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered controlled release performance. The accumulative release rate was approximately 19% within 60h in pH 7.4 PBS, while it reached 48% in pH 5.0 APS. It showed that the pH responsiveness was the main decisive factor for the release of DOX, because of the acid-cleavable Schiff base conjugation. To stimulate the practical releasing media, the in vitro release profiles of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels were also carried out at pH 7.4 with 10μM GSH (mimicking the physiological media) and pH 5.0 with 10mM GSH (mimicking the tumor microenvironment). The accumulative release rates increased to 24% and 64% within 60h. The values were higher than those in the media without GSH, indicating the reduction-responsive characteristic of the disulfide crosslinkage, which could be cleaved off in the reductive media (Zhang et al., 2017). From the DLS analysis of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels in 10mM GSH (Fig. 3b), the nanogels were broken off into smaller pieces due to the cleavage of the disulfide crosslinkage, which is beneficial to the diffusion of DOX out of the drug carriers. The results demonstrated the pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered release of the proposed DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels.

The cytocompatibility of the multi-functional COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels and the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels was evaluated in HepG2 cells after incubation for 24h using the MTT assay. The viability of the HepG2 cells decreased slightly with increasing the concentration of the COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels from 20 to 100μg/mL, and the viability was still higher than 85% at the concentration of 100μg/mL (Fig. 6 b), indicating the desirable cytocompatibility of the multi-functional COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB nanogels.

As for the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels, the viability of the HepG2 cells decreased distinctly to 46% at the concentration of 100μg/mL, demonstrating their excellent antitumor activity. Considering the DOX content of 36.7% and the accumulative release rate of about 50% within 24h, the accumulative concentration of the released DOX was calculated to be 18.8μg/mL within the incubation time of 24h at the concentration of 100μg/mL. The result showed that the anti-cancer efficiency of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels was near to the free DOX (Fig. 6a), although their gradual drug release nature (Lee, Li, & Wang, 2015).

To reveal the FR-mediated targeting function, the HepG2 cells were also incubated with different concentrations of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels (0, 20, 40, 80, 100μg/mL) in presence of 10μg/mL FA. In the case, the viability of the HepG2 cells was higher than those incubated without FA (Fig. 6b), indicating the free FA showed interference on the FR-mediated targeting function of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels, due to the competitive interaction with the over-expressed folate-receptors in the tumor cells. The results demonstrated the FR-mediated targeting function of the proposed DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels.

The IC50 (μM) values of free DOX and the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels were calculated by fitting cell viability profiles obtained from the MTT assays (Fig. 6). The IC50 of free DOX was 18.1μM in the case, while the values of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels were 61.6μM and 76.3μM respectively, incubated without or with extra 10μg/mL FA. Considering the DOX content of 36.7% and the accumulative release rate of about 50% within 24h, the cytotoxicity of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels against the HepG2 cells was obviously higher than the free DOX, even with extra 10μg/mL FA. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels was higher without free FA, in comparison with the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB+FA (extra 10μg/mL FA), also revealing the FR-mediated targeting function.

The cellular uptake experiment of the DOX-COAL-g-PEG-FA/RhB theranostic prodrug nanogels was tested by CLSM for HepG2 cells after 12h incubation with a concentration of 100μg/mL (Fig. 7 ). The HepG2 cells showed strong DOX fluorescence in cytoplasm and in nuclei after 12h, demonstrating the successful cellular uptake and/or internalization of the theranostic prodrug nanogels and DOX releasing inside the HepG2 cells. To further observe the intercellular distribution or location of the theranostic prodrug nanogels, the HepG2 cells nuclei were stained with Hoechst dye. It could be seen that DOX was released mainly in tumor cytoplasm.

In summary, cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on theranostic prodrug nanogels were designed for the tumor diagnosis and chemotherapy, based on the multi-functionalities of OAL. They were stable under physiological pH conditions in vitro with pH specific drug release because of their minimal DOX-leakage owing to the PEGylation and the acid-cleavable covalent conjugation of DOX. After internalization in tumor cells via FR-mediated endocytosis, the DOX release profile exhibited pH/reduction dual responsive intracellular triggered characteristic, due to the bioreducibly degradable disulfide crosslinking structure and acid-labile Schiff base conjugation for DOX. Furthermore, the theranostic prodrug nanogels expressed strong fluorescence only in the acidic media such as tumor intracellular microenvironment, which could be used the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells. So the proposed theranostic prodrug nanogels, showing the cancer-associated, stimuli-driven and turn-on capability for both cancer diagnosis and chemotherapy, possessed the promising potential in future cancer treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was granted financial support from the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (Grant no. NCET-09-0441).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2017.12.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
